elements.sent-2411-001: The proliferation of big data and analytic workloads has driven the need for cloud compute and cluster-based job processing.
elements.sent-2411-002: With Apache Spark, users can process terabytes of data at ease with hundreds of parallel executors.
elements.sent-2411-003: Providing low latency access to Spark clusters and sessions is a challenging problem due to the large overheads of cluster creation and session startup.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we introduce \sysname, a system for proactively provisioning compute resources to combat the aforementioned overheads.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our system (1) predicts usage patterns using an innovative hybrid Machine Learning (ML) model with low latency and high accuracy; and (2) optimizes the pool size dynamically to meet customer demand while reducing extraneous COGS.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed system auto-tunes its hyper-parameters to balance between performance and operational cost with minimal to no engineering input.
elements.sent-2411-007: Evaluated using large-scale production data, \sysname achieves up to 43\% reduction in cluster idle time compared to static pooling when targeting 99\% pool hit rate.
elements.sent-2411-008: Currently deployed in production, \sysname is on track to save tens of million dollars in COGS per year as compared to traditional pre-provisioned pools.
elements.sent-2411-010: Visit \url{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ to view a copy of this license.}

elements.sent-2411-014: Cloud computing has emerged as a top choice for executing big data analytic workloads in various business domains.
elements.sent-2411-015: To cope with rapidly increasing demand, cloud vendors (e.g., Amazon AWS~\cite{aws , Microsoft Azure~\cite{azure and Google GCP~\cite{gcp ) have funneled sizable resources into their own managed Spark services~\cite{zaharia2010spark,nghiem2016towards , including Google Cloud's Serverless Spark~\cite{spark1 , Spark through Vertex AI~\cite{spark3 , Azure HDInsight~\cite{azure1 , Azure Synapse Analytics~\cite{azure2 and AWS EMR~\cite{aws1 .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The flexibility of such cloud offerings allows users to easily lease and release compute resources as required and, consequently, enjoy potentially significant cost-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, to provide such flexibility, service providers must address various challenges with respect to resource provisioning.
elements.sent-2411-018: The implementation of multi-tenancy and scalability in such systems results in prolonged latencies in accessing clusters.
elements.sent-2411-019: With Azure Synapse~\cite{azure2 , it is common to experience a cluster initialization time of over 60 seconds.}
elements.sent-2411-020: According to Databricks~\cite{databricks , this provisioning time can be even longer than the duration of the job execution.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, proactive \edit{provisioning solutions are often challenging due to: (1) The unpredictability of user behavior and, (2) the difficulty in developing any policy that both enhances performance and decreases cost-of-goods-sold (COGS), which requires an explainable, comprehensive decision-making process in real-world production.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Accurately modeling multi-tenant cloud performance and its impact on customer experience can be complicated involving complex modeling ~\cite{chen2019cost,zhu2021kea .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Proactive auto-scaling \edit{(after application starts) has been introduced in data stream processing engines (e.g., Apache Storm~\cite{storm , Apache Flink~\cite{flink ) and network provisioning~\cite{bibal2019rlpas,zhang2017proactive to dynamically scaling up (or down) the compute resources when the workload is heavy~\cite{lee2017framework , and performance modeling is developed to ensure the QoS requirements are fulfilled ~\cite{liu2014aggressive .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Time-series forecasting is used to determine the possible repeating patterns as inputs~\cite{baldan2016forecasting .}
elements.sent-2411-025: A detailed review of similar applications in streaming systems can be seen in~\cite{lorido2014review .}
elements.sent-2411-026: For Spark~\cite{zaharia2016apache clusters, there has not been an automated solution to manage cluster provisioning.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Some vendors, like Databricks~\cite{databricks , provide mechanisms to maintain clusters until a fixed threshold of idle time is reached and offer customers instruction on ``best practices'' for managing Spark clusters on their own.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Despite its potential benefits, this approach still requires manual tuning from the user's perspective and may result in unsatisfactory performance.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we tackle the issue of improving the customer experience of waiting for the initialization of Spark clusters where a cluster needs to be prepared for a newly submitted Spark job \edit{at its startup time , which is one of the major bottlenecks for many Spark systems.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Auto-scaling relies on real-time information such as cardinality estimates, number of tasks queued, etc., which becomes available when the application starts, to adjust the number of nodes and executors.
elements.sent-2411-031: Auto-scaling during the lifetime of an application is out of scope for this paper, and \edit{for \fabric, there is a different service to scale up/down the number of nodes in the compute cluster as well as the number of executors in real time based on the incoming workload characteristics and the tracking of task execution, which include richer information about the application and the Service Level Agreements (SLAs) that need to be met~\cite{thonglek2021auto,baresi2018towards,oh2016sla .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In order to reduce the wait time for cluster initialization, we aim to use machine learning to proactively provision Spark clusters.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this presents a number of challenges, including:: \mypar{Uncertainty of user behavior [C1] Intuitively, it would be possible to provision a cluster in advance if we could accurately predict when a customer will submit a job.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, this is difficult to achieve in practice due to the high degree of uncertainty of individual user behavior.
elements.sent-2411-035: Any mismanagement of resources, including over-provisioning or under-provisioning, will result in either significant financial losses or an unsatisfactory customer experience.
elements.sent-2411-036: While most cloud operators are mandatory to meet specific service level agreements, a robust and consistent algorithm is critical.
elements.sent-2411-037: Additionally, the provisioning system must be able to adapt to a constantly changing environment by taking into account the real-time state of the system.
elements.sent-2411-038: To achieve this, it is necessary to develop and maintain a low-latency monitoring system, as well as simple and efficient algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-039: To overcome these challenges, we propose \sysname, a self-adaptive solution that proactively creates clusters based on monitoring of demand.
elements.sent-2411-040: We introduce the notion of a Spark ``live pool'', where a number of clusters are proactively created and pre-configured for various users.. [C1].
elements.sent-2411-041: Whenever a customer requests a cluster, one cluster will be immediately evicted from the pool and made available for use.
elements.sent-2411-042: At the same time, \edit{a new cluster is provisioned and added to maintain a constant cluster number, a process referred to as "re-hydration." We introduce a self-tuning system to dynamically learn the optimal pool size based on demand, considering the cost-performance trade-offs [C2, C5].}
elements.sent-2411-043: One of the biggest concerns of the live pool mechanism is the COGS.
elements.sent-2411-044: At the scale of Microsoft Fabric~\cite{fabric , we expect simple threshold-based provisioning to quickly exceed 10,000 CPU cores, resulting in tens of millions of dollars in COGS.}
elements.sent-2411-045: To address this challenge, our work proposes dynamically adjusting the size of the pool based on customer demand.
elements.sent-2411-046: We propose an efficient linear programming (LP) solution to model the two factors (performance and cost) based on the Pareto frontier to determine the optimal pool size.
elements.sent-2411-047: A self-adaptive system is proposed that automatically balances the trade-off between the cost of maintaining idle clusters and the potential for long wait times for customers.
elements.sent-2411-048: We introduce an efficient, and robust hybrid time-series forecasting algorithm of the future demand at the aggregate level with high accuracy and robustness [C1, C3, C5].
elements.sent-2411-049: The models predict future demand, measured by the cluster request rate, based on historical demand and the latest observation of the cluster creation request rate.
elements.sent-2411-050: The predictions then serve as inputs to the optimization model.
elements.sent-2411-051: With an end-to-end run time (training, inferencing, and optimizing) reduced to mere seconds, we ensure that the recommendations are always up-to-date by retraining the model with high frequency (e.g., < 5min).
elements.sent-2411-052: To improve the robustness of the model prediction [C3], we developed a new policy to smooth the input data, which significantly reduces performance regressions.
elements.sent-2411-053: We have implemented a real-time monitoring system that provides continuous inputs to constantly learn the optimal provisioning policy [C4, C5].
elements.sent-2411-054: The same dashboard is also used to evaluate the performance of the system.
elements.sent-2411-055: The end-to-end solution is \edit{lightweight and implemented in C\# as an integral part of the core Spark infrastructure [C5].}
elements.sent-2411-056: The recommendation engine can be executed in a single invocation of the pipeline, with fast and accurate recommendation generation and persistence in configuration files in mere seconds, ready to use for the pooling service.
elements.sent-2411-057: We integrate the modules with the new Fabric service, the new \msft data analytics offering that supports Spark~\cite{fabric , deployed in all production Azure regions \edit{in Nov, 2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: A simple linear programming formulation for solving the optimal pool size that captures the trade-off between improving performance and reducing COGS;.
elements.sent-2411-060: An efficient hybrid ML algorithm combining deep learning and traditional ML for predicting future demand and optimal pool size with extremely low latency;.
elements.sent-2411-061: A robust strategy to account for demand uncertainty to ensure high service level;.
elements.sent-2411-062: Deployed in production, we \edit{achieved millions of dollars in annual COGS compared to preexisting pooling.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The remaining sections are organized as follows: Section~\ref{sec:bg provides background and motivates the problem.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Section~\ref{sec:overview presents the overall design principles and architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Sections~\ref{sec:Saa and~\ref{sec:ml describe the optimization and ML modules, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Section~\ref{sec:exp goes over how we evaluated the algorithm using production data.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Section~\ref{sec:related discusses related work, and Section~\ref{sec:conclusion concludes the paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: One common issue related to Spark cluster initialization is a long wait time~\citep{databricks .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Complexity in the underlying infrastructure introduces a wide range of potential slowdowns that are difficult to detect, diagnose and mitigate.
elements.sent-2411-070: Examples of this complexity include hardware heterogeneity, unreliable network communication, and inter-node service coordination which is compounded by the strict multi-tenancy requirements in the cloud.
elements.sent-2411-071: Efforts are undergoing to reduce the tail latency of cluster initialization time, including making hedged requests~\cite{lu2014mechanisms and using tied requests \citep{dean2013tail,lu2014mechanisms .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, these approaches are not able to completely address the issue.

elements.sent-2411-074: One option here can be to add references to other papers that go into some of the infra limitations related to Spark.
elements.sent-2411-075: Another option could be to provide some type of general statement.
elements.sent-2411-076: On this platform, the typical underlying process to initiate a Spark session consists of ~60-120 seconds for the cluster creation and ~30-40 seconds for the session creation~\citep{zaharia2010spark,aleksiyants2015implementing .}
elements.sent-2411-077: When Generic Job Service requests a new cluster, \edit{ This prolonged process is primarily influenced by four main processes: VM configuration, allocation, and boosting time; stitching VMs to form Spark clusters; configuring libraries; and creating a Spark session—each taking 30-60 seconds, contributing to the extended wait time.}
elements.sent-2411-078: To minimize the latency experienced by the end-user, we propose to proactively provision Spark clusters.
elements.sent-2411-079: We categorize live pools into two buckets: session pools and cluster pools, i.e. interactive and batch mode respectively.
elements.sent-2411-080: Both consist of pooled clusters; the difference is that session pools also have an actively-running Spark session in each cluster, which we call a pooled session.
elements.sent-2411-081: Session pools are useful for notebook scenarios, when a pre-created session can be used to run a notebook instantaneously.
elements.sent-2411-082: Pooled clusters, by contrast, are useful for running batch jobs with pre-defined job definitions (e.g., a json file that describes a .jar file location, Spark configurations, etc.) and Spark sessions that require ad hoc customization.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the rest of the paper, we discuss the methodology with respect to cluster pools, though the same can be applied to session pools.
elements.sent-2411-084: Similar to the concept of inventory management in retailing~\citep{dehoratius2008retail , we maintain a constant number of resources in a given pool, and upon receiving a client request, a pre-provisioned resource can be used instantly.}
elements.sent-2411-085: To maintain the target number of resources in the pool, we send a new request, referred to as a re-hydration request, to Generic Job Service to add a new cluster or session back to the pool whenever a pooled resource is consumed or fails (due to exceeding a pre-defined lifespan or unexpected system failures).
elements.sent-2411-086: The general idea of \sysname is to dynamically determine the optimal number of resources in a pool and scale the pool up or down as needed in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-087: A larger pool can lead to wasted COGS in a low-demand scenario.
elements.sent-2411-088: On the other hand, a smaller pool has a higher likelihood of being drained out in high-demand scenarios, where numerous customers need clusters or sessions at the same time and the system does not have enough time to sufficiently replenish the pool.
elements.sent-2411-089: The client request in this situation must go through the original protracted startup process (referred to as ``on-demand").
elements.sent-2411-090: Given that ML algorithms are in general never perfect, with margins in prediction errors, the optimal provisioning strategy remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-091: In this section, we discuss the overall architecture of \sysname.
elements.sent-2411-092: Since I added some references to it, it would be good for the reader to be able to visualize how Generic Job Service interacts with it.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Sample Average Approximation (SAA) Optimizer formulates a simple linear programming problem to optimize the pool size based on input demand, which can either be historic or predicted by the ML Predictor.
elements.sent-2411-094: The optimized results are then saved as configuration files in Cosmos DB~\cite{cosmosdb (Section~\ref{sec:Saa ).}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The ML Predictor makes real-time time-series predictions by constantly fetching historic observations from the Kusto store~\cite{kusto .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Using predicted demand as opposed to historic demand helps to react more accurately to real-time changes in the system, though it can potentially lead to longer model-training latencies (Section~\ref{sec:ml ).}
elements.sent-2411-097: Generic Job Service is responsible for orchestrating Spark batch jobs and interactive sessions; providing APIs to perform CRUD operations on Spark jobs; and managing and processing Spark job-related metadata.
elements.sent-2411-098: Cluster Service is responsible for requesting virtual machines (VMs) from Azure and "stitching" them to form Spark clusters; providing APIs to perform CRUD operations on Spark clusters; and managing and processing Spark cluster-related metadata.
elements.sent-2411-099: Work Item Service is a background service that supports various workloads, including but not limited to Spark cluster and session pooling, Spark job submissions, and the \sysname infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-100: It is responsible for monitoring available work items that represent these workloads and spinning up worker processes that execute the workloads.
elements.sent-2411-101: The Intelligent Pooling Worker is responsible for periodically running the ML pipeline that includes the aforementioned SAA Optimizer and ML Predictor and persisting the recommendation files in Cosmos DB~\cite{cosmosdb .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The Pooling Worker is responsible for maintaining a target pool size by invoking Generic Job Service to create and delete resources and, if applicable, fetching the latest pool size recommendation file emitted by an Intelligent Pooling Worker.
elements.sent-2411-103: The hyper-parameter tuning module is developed to constantly fine-tune the hyper-parameters for the optimization algorithm to avoid over- or under-allocating resources by setting the tuning knobs to minimize the COGS while satisfying the SLA.
elements.sent-2411-104: It can be executed at a lower frequency while the ML pipeline runs at a higher frequency such that it captures the rapid change of the environment and adapts faster to the demand.
elements.sent-2411-105: More details on the hyper-parameter tuning process can be seen in Section~\ref{sec:tuning .}
elements.sent-2411-106: In this section, we formulate the live pool mechanism as a queuing system and propose linear programming to reach optimality.
elements.sent-2411-107: We illustrate the live pool mechanism through a graph plotting a set of cumulative values (see Figure~\ref{fig:cumulative ).}
elements.sent-2411-108: Specifically, \begin{tabular [H]{lp{6.9cm $D(t)$: the cumulative number of clusters requested by customers (demand); $N(t)$: the target pool size as a function of time that we want to maintain; $A(t)$: the cumulative number of cluster re-hydration requests made to add a cluster to the pool in order to keep it at the target pool size of $N(t)$; $\tau$: the cluster initialization time, i.e., the time lag before a cluster can be ready for use after the creation request is sent; $A'(t)$: the cumulative number of clusters ready for use.. \end{tabular.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{images/q/q2.pdf} \caption{Cumulative cluster creation number $D(t)$, cumulative re-hydration requests $A(t)$, number of clusters ready $A'(t)$ at time $t$, and pool size at time 0, $N(0)$, the total wait time (red area) for obtaining a running cluster and idle time (grey area) for clusters in the pool. } \label{fig:total_wait_time} \label{fig:cumulative}
elements.sent-2411-109: For instance, at time $t = 0$, a pool is created with $N(0)=4$ clusters, and whenever a user request for a new cluster is received, (corresponding to an increase in $D(t)$, e.g., at $t_1$), a cluster will be emitted from the pool to be used by the customer.
elements.sent-2411-110: At the same time, the pooling worker will initiate a re-hydration request to Generic Job Service to add a new cluster back to the pool (corresponding to an increase in $A(t)$).
elements.sent-2411-111: As a result, the curve of $A(t)$ can be seen as a simple ``shift-up" of the curve of $D(t)$, and the gap between them equals the target pool size $N(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Similarly, because cluster creation takes time, the cumulative number of clusters that are ready to use, $A’(t)$, is a ``shift-right" of the number of requests made, $A(t)$, by the cluster creation latency, $\tau$: \begin{equation}A(t) = D(t) + N(t),\quad \forall t \label{eq:cons1} A'(t) = A(t-\tau),\quad \forall t\geq \tau \label{eq:cons2} A'(t) = N(0),\quad \forall t < \tau. \label{eq:cons3}\end{equation} Assuming a first-come-first-serve rule, the clusters in the pool will be acquired by customers based on the user request arrival time.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}A(t) = D(t) + N(t),\quad \forall t \label{eq:cons1} A'(t) = A(t-\tau),\quad \forall t\geq \tau \label{eq:cons2} A'(t) = N(0),\quad \forall t < \tau. \label{eq:cons3}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-113: For instance, in Figure~\ref{fig:cumulative , the first four clusters in the pool will be used by the first four requests that are received (blue, yellow, grey and orange respectively).}
elements.sent-2411-114: The fifth created cluster is triggered by the arrival of the blue request at $t_1$, and is not ready until $t_3$.
elements.sent-2411-115: It will be used by the fifth request received at $t_2$.
elements.sent-2411-116: There are two factors to optimize for:.
elements.sent-2411-117: The total idle time pooled clusters are alive and unused by customers; and.
elements.sent-2411-118: The total wait time for customers (when the pool is drained out and the customer must wait for the full cluster startup duration).
elements.sent-2411-119: In Figure \ref{fig:total_wait_time we show the one-to-one mapping between a created cluster and the request that will use the corresponding cluster based on a first-come-first-serve (FCFS) rule.}
elements.sent-2411-120: We observe that whenever $A'(t)>D(t)$, idle time occurs, and whenever $A'(t)<D(t)$, wait time occurs.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, at time $t=0$, four clusters have been created, and they will be used by the first four requests that come in (blue, yellow, grey and orange).
elements.sent-2411-122: For those clusters, their aggregate idle time equals the area of the gap between the curves of $A'(t)$ and $D(t)$, highlighted in grey.
elements.sent-2411-123: However, the fifth cluster creation request on curve $A(t)$ (marked in blue) was not ready until $t_3$ while the fifth customer request on curve $D(t)$ occurs at $t_2$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Therefore, for the fifth request, the customer has to wait for $t_3-t_2$, and the wait time was highlighted in red (similar for the sixth, seventh and eighth requests in yellow, grey, and orange respectively)\footnote{Note that currently, when a pool is drained out, ``on-demand'' cluster creation requests will be sent to accommodate the cluster requests, and their wait time becomes $\tau$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: The clusters being ready at $t_3$ and onwards will be served to later requests.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this case, the FCFS rule is violated as the order of using the clusters is modified.
elements.sent-2411-127: In this work, we still assume FCFS for simplicity as an approximation of this mechanism.. .
elements.sent-2411-128: In sum, the total wait time of customers is the red area where $A'(t)<D(t)$, and the total idle time of clusters in the pool is the grey area where $A'(t)>D(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-129: With this, we can estimate the optimal pool size where both the total wait time and the idle time are minimized.
elements.sent-2411-130: Specifically, we can leverage linear programming with minimization as the objective to calculate the areas: \begin{equation}\Delta^{+}(t) \geq A'(t) - D(t),\quad \forall t\label{eq:cons4} \Delta^{+}(t) \geq 0\quad, \forall t \Delta^{-}(t) \geq D(t) - A'(t),\quad\forall t \Delta^{-}(t) \geq 0,\quad \forall t. \label{eq:cons7}\end{equation} If the objective function involves minimizing $\Delta^{+ (t)$ and $\Delta^{- (t)$, one can prove that in the optimal solution, if $A'(t) \geq D(t)$, $\Delta^{+ (t) = A'(t) - D(t)$ and $\Delta^{- (t)=0$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\Delta^{+}(t) \geq A'(t) - D(t),\quad \forall t\label{eq:cons4} \Delta^{+}(t) \geq 0\quad, \forall t \Delta^{-}(t) \geq D(t) - A'(t),\quad\forall t \Delta^{-}(t) \geq 0,\quad \forall t. \label{eq:cons7}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: And the sum of $\Delta^{+}$ and $\Delta^{-}$ calculates the total area of grey (idle time) and red (wait time) respectively.
elements.sent-2411-133: And the constraints are all linear.
elements.sent-2411-134: Based on the above discussion, we can formulate the optimization program with the objective to minimize the total cost (considered as a weighted sum of the wait time and idle time).
elements.sent-2411-135: We use the sample average approximation (SAA) method~\cite{kleywegt2002sample based on the input demand data to minimize the expected total cost over the whole observed period.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Denote $\alpha$ and $\beta$ the hyper-parameters representing the penalty of having long idle time versus wait time, a larger $\alpha$ will result in an optimal solution trying to minimize the idle time more than wait time, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-137: By changing the hyper-parameter values, one can achieve the full Pareto curve~\cite{floratou2017dhalion, pareto of the trade-off between idle time and wait time.}
elements.sent-2411-138: The detailed formulation is as follows: \begin{equation}\min \alpha \cdot\sum_{t}{\Delta^{+}(t)} + \beta \cdot \sum_{t}{\Delta^{-}(t)} \label{eq:op1} ~s.t.,\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad~\eqref{eq:cons1} - \eqref{eq:cons7}. \nonumber \qedhere\end{equation} With this minimization formulation, for the optimal solution, $\Delta^{+ (t)$ equals the number of idle clusters at time $t$, and $\Delta^{-}$ the queued demand.}
elements.sent-2411-140: For the maximum number of requests made, one can add a constraint with the maximum number of requests per time interval, MAX NEW REQUEST: \begin{equation}N(t) - N(t-1)\leq MAX NEW REQUEST \quad \forall t\ge1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-141: For the analyzed system, we also added the following constraints: \begin{equation}MIN POOL SIZE\leq N(t)\leq MAX POOL SIZE \quad \forall t\ge1,\label{eq:minmax} N(t) = N(\nint{t / STABLENESS} * STABLENESS) \quad \forall t\ge1.\label{eq:stable}\end{equation} where Constraint~\eqref{eq:minmax sets the minimum and maximum pool size (i.e., MIN POOL SIZE and MAX POOL SIZE), and Constraints~\eqref{eq:stable ensures that the pool size is stable for $\Delta t=\text{STABLENESS}$ intervals.}}

elements.sent-2411-243: In this work, we focus on cluster/session pooling.
elements.sent-2411-244: In this work, we propose “pooling” (proactively provisioning) Spark clusters and sessions to eliminate the initial startup latencies.
elements.sent-2411-245: Intelligent Pooling involves predicting customer demand through an innovative hybrid machine learning model and dynamically adjusting the pool size to meet customer demand while reducing extraneous COGS using linear programming with low latency and high robustness.

elements.sent-2411-249: We sincerely thank the reviewers for their critical assessments and suggestions to help improve our paper.
elements.sent-2411-250: We addressed all the comments in the revised draft.
elements.sent-2411-252: Added detailed clarification for design choices of dynamic pooling versus other solutions such as managed Spark services and VM pooling in Section 2.
elements.sent-2411-253: Clarified Fabric's design choices of using a multi-tenant managed service versus dedicated pools in Section 2.
elements.sent-2411-254: Clarified the difference between autoscaling and intelligent pooling in Section 1.
elements.sent-2411-255: Added a discussion on the cluster creation time and its bottleneck in Section 2.
elements.sent-2411-256: Added details on the ML model and the pros and cons for different algorithms in Section 7.
elements.sent-2411-257: Discussed the fault tolerance guarantee of the system.
elements.sent-2411-258: Added experiment results for scalability of all ML algorithms in Section 7.
elements.sent-2411-259: We would like to emphasize that autoscaling and intelligent pooling serve distinct purposes and utilize different inputs.
elements.sent-2411-260: In the case of Fabric, the autoscaling mechanism is enabled during provisioning, where a cluster is initially provisioned with a relatively small size, e.g., 1 node.
elements.sent-2411-261: Once the customer application starts, the cluster dynamically scales up or down based on real-time information, such as the number of queued tasks.
elements.sent-2411-262: This autoscaling solution addresses the challenge of adjusting the number of executors and nodes for a cluster during the application's runtime.
elements.sent-2411-263: It's important to note that this aspect is beyond the scope of our paper.
elements.sent-2411-264: In contrast, the intelligent pooling solution focuses on reducing the waiting time for cluster initialization (around 3 minutes) before the application begins by proactively creating and provisioning clusters in the pool.
elements.sent-2411-265: The objective is to determine the optimal number of clusters in the pool.
elements.sent-2411-266: One of the main challenges with this pooling mechanism is the associated cost, as idled clusters continue to incur expenses while sitting in the pool without being acquired by any applications.
elements.sent-2411-267: Therefore, we propose dynamically tuning the pool size (i.e., the number of clusters in the pool) in a manner similar to an inventory problem, aiming to reduce costs according to demand, specifically cluster request rates.
elements.sent-2411-268: Additionally, to enhance the understanding of dynamic pooling and the resulting cost savings, we introduced Figure 1 in Section 2.. \myparr{MR2: Scalability.}
elements.sent-2411-269: The scalability of the problem encompasses three key aspects: (1) ML training, (2) ML prediction, and (3) linear programming (LP).
elements.sent-2411-270: In Section 7.4, we examined the scalability of the ML training process, identified as the primary bottleneck in the pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-271: Our assertion is that augmenting training data could potentially improve prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-272: Notably, for the selected algorithms, the inference time is considerably short compared to the training process.
elements.sent-2411-273: Regarding linear programming (LP), increasing the training data size does not introduce latency issues.
elements.sent-2411-274: The input to LP is the predicted cluster request rate for a fixed time window in the future, consistently comprising 120 data points (representing a 1-hour ahead demand prediction).
elements.sent-2411-275: The optimization program, executed with respect to this 1-hour demand, recommends the optimal pool size for this time window.
elements.sent-2411-276: Even with larger input data, such as a 14-day history for the ML training pipeline, the latency of linear programming remains unchanged, as the predicted demand input to LP is always for the 1-hour window.
elements.sent-2411-277: We further evaluated the linear programming with a 1-week ahead demand prediction as input, consisting of over 20,000 data points.
elements.sent-2411-278: The latency remains minimal (0.28s) based on our formulation as presented in Equation (8).
elements.sent-2411-279: As one of the reviewer suggested, we added experiments for scalability evaluation for all ML algorithms in Figure 6.
elements.sent-2411-280: These models were specifically chosen as they each represent a different category: SSA is a traditional ML-based approach, TST is a transformer-based deep learning approach, mWDN is a wavelet decomposition-based approach, and Inception Time is a 1D convolution model.
elements.sent-2411-281: The mWDN model utilizes an Inception Time backbone.
elements.sent-2411-282: In this research, we proposed an innovative hybrid model (SSA+) that achieves near-optimal accuracy with high efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-283: The hyperparameters used are reported in Section 7.2.
elements.sent-2411-284: We thank the reviewer for this question.
elements.sent-2411-285: Potential system failures stem from two sources: (1) inference pipeline failure and (2) other pooling worker failures.
elements.sent-2411-286: To address these concerns, we've implemented the following mechanisms: \myparr{Algorithm failure handling: To enhance fault tolerance for the inference algorithm, we've implemented two additional measures: (1) one single algorithm run generates recommendations for the next hour, and we execute the algorithm with shorter intervals, e.g., 30 min.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Consequently, even if the algorithm fails once, the system retains the previous recommendation output, albeit slightly outdated.
elements.sent-2411-288: When a worker is assigned, the document enters a ``locked'' state.
elements.sent-2411-289: Assignments have a time-to-live (TTL), and upon expiration, the contract between the worker and the work item is refreshed to ``available''.
elements.sent-2411-290: This ensures periodic checks to confirm consistent assignment to healthy workers.
elements.sent-2411-291: The Arbitrator service oversees this process and restarts pooling tasks upon detecting an unhealthy worker.
elements.sent-2411-292: In summary, each pooling task is leased to a worker, and upon lease expiration, it undergoes refreshment, ensuring regular health checks for all involved workers.
elements.sent-2411-293: Unhealthy workers are promptly replaced with healthy ones.
elements.sent-2411-294: One thing to note is the Fabric Spark workloads have 2 flavors: batch job and interactive job.
elements.sent-2411-295: Fabric offers both data engineering and data science experiences, with customers often utilizing interactive notebooks to submit jobs for extended durations.
elements.sent-2411-296: We actively focus on optimizing the balance between time spent pooling resources and customers utilizing computes to efficiently manage costs.
elements.sent-2411-297: Regarding multi-tenancy, Fabric operates as a multi-tenant managed Spark service, with a security boundary scoped to individual users.
elements.sent-2411-298: Once a cluster is acquired, the customer's identity is acquired to access external resources.
elements.sent-2411-299: For cases where customers want to reuse the same spark session, Fabric provides features like High Concurrency, enabling users to employ the same spark job across multiple notebooks.
elements.sent-2411-300: As a managed Spark service, Fabric restricts customers from explicitly creating clusters.
elements.sent-2411-301: Instead, they utilize the semantics of running Spark job targeting starter pools.
elements.sent-2411-302: The security boundary in Fabric is established at the Spark cluster layer.
elements.sent-2411-303: Clusters are created by stitching up VMs, and provisioning VMs to customers delivers a higher level of isolation and security.
elements.sent-2411-304: VM pooling, as a result, adds complexity, necessitating the task of transferring a pooled VM from the pooled network to be injected into another VM's network.
elements.sent-2411-305: It is outside our current consideration.
elements.sent-2411-307: Please kindly refer to the response to MR1.. \myparr{R1.}
elements.sent-2411-308: D2: Motivating the pooling mechanism with its overhead.
elements.sent-2411-309: For Fabric, we create only two pools per region (one for session and one for cluster).
elements.sent-2411-310: The update of the pool happens very infrequently, occurring only with significant software updates for the service fabric.
elements.sent-2411-311: Consequently, the overhead is minimal as the creation of the pool is not frequent.
elements.sent-2411-312: Similar concepts of pooling have been introduced for other cloud services~\cite{wood2011cloudnet, databricks .}
elements.sent-2411-313: In this work, we propose leveraging ML to dynamically tune the size to optimize operational costs.
elements.sent-2411-314: D3: The impact of different frequencies of running the ML pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-315: For Fabric, our goal is to run the pipeline as frequently as possible to capture any unexpected demand changes.
elements.sent-2411-316: While running the pipeline consumes compute resources, the corresponding cost increase is not very significant, given the relatively small number of pools, compared to other operational costs.. \myparr{R1.}
elements.sent-2411-318: Please refer to the response to MR2.. \myparr{R1.}
elements.sent-2411-319: D5: Values for MAX NEW REQUEST, MIN/MAX POOL SIZE.
elements.sent-2411-320: For the production pipeline, we set the MAX NEW REQUEST to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-321: The min/max pool size is determined per region based on the capacity limit.
elements.sent-2411-322: The min size ranges from 1 to 8, and the max pool size can be up to 100.
elements.sent-2411-323: Additionally, following our recommendation, if the max pool size constraint is reached, we suggest the capacity team reevaluate the setting to consider allocating more compute resources for the corresponding region.
elements.sent-2411-325: For evaluation, we aggregate the demand based on 30s intervals.
elements.sent-2411-326: Therefore, the time series will have a length of 2880 for an input of 1 day's data.
elements.sent-2411-327: In Figure 5, input data with a 40-day duration will have 115,200 input data points.
elements.sent-2411-328: The hybrid SSA+ model stands as a primary methodological contribution in this work.
elements.sent-2411-329: We selected various state-of-the-art ML models as benchmarks, each representing a distinct category: SSA as a traditional ML-based approach, TST as a transformer-based deep learning approach, mWDN as a wavelet decomposition-based approach, and Inception Time as a 1D convolution model.
elements.sent-2411-330: Our observations revealed that DNN models offer flexibility with customized loss functions, allowing tailoring to different performance and cost preferences for demand prediction—whether conservative or aggressive.
elements.sent-2411-331: However, their training times can be notably long, as depicted in Figure 6.
elements.sent-2411-332: On the other hand, traditional models like SSA boast swift training times but lack the flexibility to provide different percentiles for demand prediction.
elements.sent-2411-333: To address this, we proposed the innovative hybrid algorithm, SSA+, combining the strengths of DNN and traditional ML models.
elements.sent-2411-334: SSA+ achieves comparable optimality to DNN models (as seen in Figure 5) while maintaining very low latency compared to simple traditional ML models (see Figure 6).
elements.sent-2411-337: In this study, all the ML models are time-series models, and the input is the time-series representing the demand by time, i.e., the number of clusters requested per interval (e.g., 30s).
elements.sent-2411-339: In our solution, as the total end-to-end training, inferencing, and optimizing time is very short (merely seconds), the pipeline can be rerun very frequently to capture the dynamic changes in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-340: In production, we let the pipeline execute every 30 minutes and generate the recommendation of the pool size for the next hour.

elements.sent-2411-344: In this work, we proposed the innovative SSA+ method that is both efficient (significantly faster than DNN models) and flexible and is thus suitable for production pipelines with a simple single-node compute (6 vCores and 64 GB RAM) that hosts the lightweight intelligent pooling worker in the service layer of Fabric (see Figure 2).
elements.sent-2411-345: Moreover, the linear programming solution we formulated is efficient and accurate with very low latency.
elements.sent-2411-346: The end-to-end pipeline has been deployed in production for several months without any significant impact or extra overhead for the rest of the services in the same layer.
elements.sent-2411-348: This is a crucial aspect of the system.

elements.sent-2411-351: We highlighted the limitations of different ML algorithms based on our observations in Section 5.2 and demonstrated our rationale behind the choices of different algorithms and how it leads to the new hybrid method in Section 5.3.

elements.sent-2411-355: Yes, as pointed out by the reviewer, for Fabric, we provide only one fixed cluster size, such as 1 node with 3 executors, as the initial size of the clusters in the pool.
elements.sent-2411-356: As the customer application starts, auto-scaling handles changes in the cluster size.
elements.sent-2411-357: One avenue for future work is to establish a reasonable fixed set of cluster sizes to start with, further tailoring them to different customer needs.

elements.sent-2411-362: In our evaluation, mWDN exhibited the best accuracy overall and significantly better latency than TST and InceptionTime across different pools.
elements.sent-2411-363: As suggested by the reviewer, we conducted a scaling test for all the algorithms and reported the latency in Figure 6.
